summary:
Abu Rayhan al-Biruni (973–1048 AD) was one of the greatest scholars of the [[event:2135|Islamic Golden Age]] — a polymath of extraordinary breadth who made fundamental contributions to astronomy, mathematics, physics, geography, anthropology, pharmacology, and the comparative study of religions. Born in Khwarazm (modern Uzbekistan), al-Biruni spent decades at the court of [[event:2929|Mahmud of Ghazni]], accompanying the sultan's invasions of India and using the access to Indian scholars and texts to produce his masterwork, the 'Kitab ul-Hind' (Book of India, 1017–1030 AD) — the most systematic, respectful, and intellectually sophisticated study of a foreign civilization produced in the medieval Islamic world.

Al-Biruni's 'Kitab ul-Hind' stands apart from contemporary accounts of India by its method: rather than dismissing Hindu science, philosophy, and religion as inferior, al-Biruni learned Sanskrit, engaged directly with Indian mathematical and astronomical texts, compared Indian and Greek thought systematically, and tried to explain Indian beliefs and practices on their own terms — an approach that anticipates modern comparative anthropology by eight centuries. In astronomy and mathematics, al-Biruni made precise calculations of the Earth's circumference using a novel geometric method, calculated latitude and longitude with remarkable accuracy, discussed the possibility of the Earth's rotation, and produced a comprehensive study of Indian astronomy that identified the equivalences between Indian and Greek astronomical concepts. His work in mineralogy and pharmacology similarly combined encyclopedic knowledge with original observation. Al-Biruni remains arguably the most universally gifted scholar of the medieval period in any civilization.
